section.title: The Vision of the New Temple
section.sectionRationale: After the fall of Jerusalem's temple and the promises of restoration, Ezekiel receives the most sustained architectural vision in Scripture: a new temple, measured room by room, gate by gate, court by court, by a heavenly guide carrying a measuring rod. The sheer weight of detail itself preaches: God's presence among His people will be no careless afterthought but exactly ordered, spacious, and holy.
section.thesis: In a vision overflowing with precise measurements, God shows His exiled people that His dwelling among them will be real, ordered, and holy, a pattern whose deepest meaning is fulfilled not in stone but in Christ, the true temple, in the church, and finally in a new creation where God's presence needs no temple at all.
section.frameNote: Eleven scenes follow the measuring man from the outer wall inward: the east gate and outer court, the inner court and its gates, the tables and priestly chambers, the temple portico, the outer sanctuary and Most Holy Place, the side rooms, the carved cherubim and palm trees, the priests' holy chambers, and finally the great measured square that separates the holy from the common.

units.label: The man with the measuring reed
units.note: brought to a very high mountain

verses.text: In the twenty-fifth year of our exile, at the beginning of the year, on the tenth day of the month, in the fourteenth year after Jerusalem had been struck down, on that very day the hand of the LORD was upon me, and He took me there.
In visions of God He took me to the land of Israel and set me on a very high mountain, on whose southern slope was a structure that resembled a city.
So He took me there, and I saw a man whose appearance was like bronze. He was standing in the gateway with a linen cord and a {{qaneh|measuring rod}} in his hand.

“Son of man,” he said to me, “look with your eyes, hear with your ears, and pay attention to everything I am going to show you, for that is why you have been brought here. Report to the house of Israel everything you see.”

units.label: The outer wall and the east gate

verses.text: And I saw a wall surrounding the temple area. Now the length of the measuring rod in the man’s hand was six long cubits (each measuring a cubit and a handbreadth), and he measured the wall to be one rod thick and one rod high.
Then he came to the gate facing east and climbed its steps. He measured the threshold of the gate to be one rod deep.
Each gate chamber was one rod long and one rod wide, and there were five cubits between the gate chambers. The inner threshold of the gate by the portico facing inward was one rod deep.
Then he measured the portico of the gateway inside;
it was eight cubits deep, and its jambs were two cubits thick. And the portico of the gateway faced the temple.
There were three gate chambers on each side of the east gate, each with the same measurements, and the gateposts on either side also had the same measurements.
And he measured the width of the gateway entrance to be ten cubits, and its length was thirteen cubits.
In front of each gate chamber was a wall one cubit high, and the gate chambers were six cubits square.
Then he measured the gateway from the roof of one gate chamber to the roof of the opposite one; the distance was twenty-five cubits from doorway to doorway.
Next he measured the gateposts to be sixty cubits high. The gateway extended around to the gatepost of the courtyard.
And the distance from the entrance of the gateway to the far end of its inner portico was fifty cubits.

The gate chambers and their side pillars had beveled windows all around the inside of the gateway. The porticos also had windows all around on the inside. Each side pillar was decorated with palm trees.

units.label: The outer court and its north and south gates

verses.text: Then he brought me into the outer court, and there were chambers and a pavement laid out all around the court. Thirty chambers faced the pavement,
which flanked the gateways and corresponded to the length of the gates; this was the lower pavement.
Then he measured the distance from the front of the lower gateway to the outside of the inner court; it was a hundred cubits on the east side as well as on the north.
He also measured the length and width of the gateway of the outer court facing north.
Its three gate chambers on each side, its side pillars, and its portico all had the same measurements as the first gate: fifty cubits long and twenty-five cubits wide.
Its windows, portico, and palm trees had the same measurements as those of the gate facing east. Seven steps led up to it, with its portico opposite them.
There was a gate to the inner court facing the north gate, just as there was on the east. He measured the distance from gateway to gateway to be a hundred cubits.
Then he led me to the south side, and I saw a gateway facing south. He measured its side pillars and portico, and they had the same measurements as the others.
Both the gateway and its portico had windows all around, like the other windows. It was fifty cubits long and twenty-five cubits wide.
Seven steps led up to it, and its portico was opposite them; it had palm trees on its side pillars, one on each side.
The inner court also had a gate facing south, and he measured the distance from gateway to gateway toward the south to be a hundred cubits.

units.label: The inner court and its gates

verses.text: Next he brought me into the inner court through the south gate, and he measured the south gate; it had the same measurements as the others.
Its gate chambers, side pillars, and portico had the same measurements as the others. Both the gateway and its portico had windows all around; it was fifty cubits long and twenty-five cubits wide.
(The porticoes around the inner court were twenty-five cubits long and five cubits deep.)
Its portico faced the outer court, and its side pillars were decorated with palm trees. Eight steps led up to it.
And he brought me to the inner court on the east side, and he measured the gateway; it had the same measurements as the others.
Its gate chambers, side pillars, and portico had the same measurements as the others. Both the gateway and its portico had windows all around. It was fifty cubits long and twenty-five cubits wide.
Its portico faced the outer court, and its side pillars were decorated with palm trees on each side. Eight steps led up to it.
Then he brought me to the north gate and measured it. It had the same measurements as the others,
as did its gate chambers, side pillars, and portico. It also had windows all around. It was fifty cubits long and twenty-five cubits wide.
Its portico faced the outer court, and its side pillars were decorated with palm trees on each side. Eight steps led up to it.
There was a chamber with a doorway by the portico in each of the inner gateways. There the burnt offering was to be washed.

units.label: Tables for sacrifice and chambers for the priests
units.note: sons of Zadok who minister before the LORD

verses.text: Inside the portico of the gateway were two tables on each side, on which the burnt offerings, sin offerings, and guilt offerings were to be slaughtered.
Outside, as one goes up to the entrance of the north gateway, there were two tables on one side and two more tables on the other side of the gate’s portico.
So there were four tables inside the gateway and four outside, eight tables in all, on which the sacrifices were to be slaughtered.

There were also four tables of dressed stone for the burnt offering, each a cubit and a half long, a cubit and a half wide, and a cubit high. On these were placed the utensils used to slaughter the burnt offerings and the other sacrifices.
The double-pronged hooks, each a handbreadth long, were fastened all around the inside of the room, and the flesh of the offering was to be placed on the tables.
Outside the inner gate, within the inner court, were two chambers, one beside the north gate and facing south, and another beside the south gate and facing north.
Then the man said to me: “The chamber that faces south is for the priests who keep charge of the temple,
and the chamber that faces north is for the priests who keep charge of the altar. These are the sons of Zadok, the only Levites who may approach the LORD to minister before Him.”
Next he measured the court. It was square, a hundred cubits long and a hundred cubits wide. And the altar was in front of the temple.

units.label: The portico of the temple
units.note: steps leading up to the house itself

verses.text: Then he brought me to the portico of the temple and measured the side pillars of the portico to be five cubits on each side. The width of the gateway was fourteen cubits and its sidewalls were three cubits on either side.
The portico was twenty cubits wide and twelve cubits deep, and ten steps led up to it. There were columns by the side pillars, one on each side.

units.label: The outer sanctuary and the Most Holy Place

verses.text: Then the man brought me into the outer sanctuary and measured the side pillars to be six cubits wide on each side.

The width of the entrance was ten cubits, and the sides of the entrance were five cubits on each side. He also measured the length of the outer sanctuary to be forty cubits, and the width to be twenty cubits.
And he went into the inner sanctuary and measured the side pillars at the entrance to be two cubits wide. The entrance was six cubits wide, and the walls on each side were seven cubits wide.
Then he measured the room adjacent to the inner sanctuary to be twenty cubits long and twenty cubits wide. And he said to me, “This is the Most Holy Place.”

units.label: The side rooms and the surrounding structure
units.note: a raised base and an ascending stairway

verses.text: Next he measured the wall of the temple to be six cubits thick, and the width of each side room around the temple was four cubits.

The side rooms were arranged one above another in three levels of thirty rooms each. There were ledges all around the wall of the temple to serve as supports for the side rooms, so that the supports would not be fastened into the wall of the temple itself.

The side rooms surrounding the temple widened at each successive level, because the structure surrounding the temple ascended by stages corresponding to the narrowing of the temple wall as it rose upward. And so a stairway went up from the lowest story to the highest, through the middle one.
I saw that the temple had a raised base all around it, forming the foundation of the side rooms. It was the full length of a rod, six long cubits.
The outer wall of the side rooms was five cubits thick, and the open area between the side rooms of the temple
and the outer chambers was twenty cubits wide all around the temple.
The side rooms opened into this area, with one entrance on the north and another on the south. The open area was five cubits wide all around.
Now the building that faced the temple courtyard on the west was seventy cubits wide, and the wall of the building was five cubits thick all around, with a length of ninety cubits.
Then he measured the temple to be a hundred cubits long, and the temple courtyard and the building with its walls were also a hundred cubits long.
The width of the temple courtyard on the east, including the front of the temple, was a hundred cubits.

units.label: Cherubim, palm trees, and the wooden altar

verses.text: Next he measured the length of the building facing the temple courtyard at the rear of the temple, including its galleries on each side; it was a hundred cubits. The outer sanctuary, the inner sanctuary, and the porticoes facing the court,

as well as the thresholds and the beveled windows and the galleries all around with their three levels opposite the threshold, were overlaid with wood on all sides. They were paneled from the ground to the windows, and the windows were covered.
In the space above the outside of the entrance to the inner sanctuary on all the walls, spaced evenly around the inner and outer sanctuary,
were alternating carved {{kerub|cherubim}} and palm trees. Each cherub had two faces:
the face of a man was toward the palm tree on one side, and the face of a young lion was toward the palm tree on the other side. They were carved all the way around the temple.
Cherubim and palm trees were carved on the wall of the outer sanctuary from the floor to the space above the entrance.
The outer sanctuary had a rectangular doorframe, and the doorframe of the sanctuary was similar.
There was an altar of wood three cubits high and two cubits square. Its corners, base, and sides were of wood. And the man told me, “This is the table that is before the LORD.”
Both the outer sanctuary and the inner sanctuary had double doors,
and each door had two swinging panels. There were two panels for one door and two for the other.
Cherubim and palm trees like those on the walls were carved on the doors of the outer sanctuary, and there was a wooden canopy outside, on the front of the portico.
There were beveled windows and palm trees on the sidewalls of the portico. The side rooms of the temple also had canopies.

units.label: Chambers for the holy offerings
units.note: where the priests eat what is most holy

verses.text: Then the man led me out northward into the outer court, and he brought me to the group of chambers opposite the temple courtyard and the outer wall on the north side.
The building with the door facing north was a hundred cubits long and fifty cubits wide.
Gallery faced gallery in three levels opposite the twenty cubits that belonged to the inner court and opposite the pavement that belonged to the outer court.
In front of the chambers was an inner walkway ten cubits wide and a hundred cubits long. Their doors were on the north.
Now the upper chambers were smaller because the galleries took more space from the chambers on the lower and middle floors of the building.
For they were arranged in three stories, and unlike the courts, they had no pillars. So the upper chambers were set back further than the lower and middle floors.
An outer wall in front of the chambers was fifty cubits long and ran parallel to the chambers and the outer court.
For the chambers on the outer court were fifty cubits long, while those facing the temple were a hundred cubits long.
And below these chambers was the entrance on the east side as one enters them from the outer court.
On the south side along the length of the wall of the outer court were chambers adjoining the courtyard and opposite the building,
with a passageway in front of them, just like the chambers that were on the north. They had the same length and width, with similar exits and dimensions.
And corresponding to the doors of the chambers that were facing south, there was a door in front of the walkway that was parallel to the wall extending eastward.

Then the man said to me, “The north and south chambers facing the temple courtyard are the {{qodesh|holy}} chambers where the priests who approach the LORD will eat the most holy offerings. There they will place the most holy offerings, the grain offerings, the sin offerings, and the guilt offerings, for the place is holy.

Once the priests have entered the holy area, they must not go out into the outer court until they have left behind the garments in which they minister, for these are holy. They are to put on other clothes before they approach the places that are for the people.”

units.label: The measured square: holy and common
units.note: a boundary five hundred cubits wide

verses.text: Now when the man had finished measuring the interior of the temple area, he led me out by the gate that faced east, and he measured the area all around:
With a measuring rod he measured the east side to be five hundred cubits long.
He measured the north side to be five hundred cubits long.
He measured the south side to be five hundred cubits long.
And he came around and measured the west side to be five hundred cubits long.
So he measured the area on all four sides. It had a wall all around, five hundred cubits long and five hundred cubits wide, to separate the holy from the {{chol|common}}.

qaneh.gloss: A reed, stalk, or measuring rod. The heavenly guide's instrument of precise measurement, a visible sign that God's dwelling is exactly ordered, nothing left to chance or approximation.

kerub.gloss: An angelic being, a cherub. Carved figures line the temple walls beside palm trees, echoing the cherubim who guarded Eden's entrance and overshadowed the ark, guardians now built permanently into the very architecture of God's house.

qodesh.gloss: Holy, set apart, sacred. The word that names the chambers, the offerings, and the whole mountain of the temple, marking off a space and a way of life wholly devoted to God.

chol.gloss: Common, ordinary, not sacred. Paired with qodesh at the very end of the vision's measurements, it fixes a clear boundary between the space set apart for God and everyday life.

basic.context: Fourteen years after Jerusalem fell, and twenty-five years into the exile, Ezekiel is taken in vision to a very high mountain in Israel, where a bronze-like man with a measuring rod walks him, gate by gate and room by room, through an enormous, unbuilt temple. Every wall, gateway, chamber, and table is precisely measured, culminating in a five-hundred-cubit square that fixes a boundary between the holy and the common. It is the longest sustained architectural vision in the Bible, and its very thoroughness is the message: after the old temple was defiled and destroyed, God shows His people, down to the cubit, that His dwelling among them is no careless afterthought.
basic.hermeneutics: This vision uses the same 'as it were' apocalyptic idiom found throughout Ezekiel, now applied to architecture rather than storm and throne. Interpreters have long debated how literally to press its measurements, since some details do not straightforwardly match any temple ever built, including the Second Temple or Herod's. Readers should hold the specific numbers with humility while trusting the vision's clear theological center: God's presence is coming back, in careful, holy order.
basic.originalLanguages: The guide carries a qaneh, a measuring reed, the instrument of a God who leaves nothing to chance. Carved kerubim, cherubim, alternate with palm trees on every wall, echoing Eden's guardians. The vision moves steadily toward one word, qodesh, holy, set apart, and closes by fixing its opposite, chol, the common, at a firm distance from it.
basic.typology: The measuring rod that fixes every dimension anticipates the measuring of the new Jerusalem in Revelation 21:15-17. The carved cherubim guarding the sanctuary recall Eden's cherubim and anticipate a house where access to God is no longer barred. And the whole ordered structure points toward Christ, who called His own body 'this temple' (John 2:19-21), the true meeting place of God and man that no measured building could ever be.
basic.christ: John records Jesus pointing to His own body as the true temple, 'destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up,' John 2:19-21, so that Ezekiel's measured house finds its deepest fulfillment not in stone but in a person. Paul then extends the pattern to the church, 'built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit,' Ephesians 2:19-22, and John finally sees a city with no temple at all, 'because the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb are its temple,' Revelation 21:22.
basic.god: God is a God of order, not chaos, taking such evident care over gates, chambers, tables, and thresholds that no detail is beneath His notice. He is also a God who guards holiness fiercely, since the vision moves toward one line, 'to separate the holy from the common' (42:20), yet its whole purpose is to prepare a way for Him to dwell among His people again.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 6-7 describes Solomon's temple, the pattern this vision both echoes and surpasses. John 2:19-21 identifies Jesus' body as the true temple. Ephesians 2:19-22 extends the temple pattern to the church. Revelation 21:15-22 measures the new Jerusalem and finds no temple there at all, for God and the Lamb are its temple.
basic.oneStory: From the tabernacle in the wilderness to Solomon's temple to this measured vision, Scripture keeps returning to one question: how can a holy God dwell among an unholy people? Ezekiel's temple answers with careful, hopeful detail, and the rest of the Bible narrows that answer to one person, Christ, in whom God's presence permanently dwells, before opening out again into the whole church and finally into a new creation where every corner is holy and no temple building is needed at all.

context.literary: The longest single vision in Ezekiel, told with an almost liturgical rhythm of measurement after measurement, gate after gate, moving steadily inward from the outer wall to the Most Holy Place and then back out to the great surrounding square. The repetition itself is the literary device: order upon order upon order, building confidence that this house has been planned with total care.
context.historical: Given to exiles whose temple lay in ruins and whose worship had been stripped away, this vision answers despair with detail: a temple already fully designed, awaiting only the day of its realization, assuring the exiles that God's plans for His dwelling among them were neither abandoned nor vague.
context.canonical: This vision stands behind the New Testament's repeated use of temple imagery for Christ and the church, and directly anticipates the measuring of the new Jerusalem in Revelation 21, where the temple pattern is finally fulfilled by God's own unmediated presence.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure exiles who had lost their temple that God's dwelling among them would return in a form more ordered, more holy, and more secure than before, and to teach, through the vision's careful gradations, the seriousness of approaching a holy God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes a revealed pattern; whether it prescribes an exact future building program is precisely the point of ongoing debate among faithful readers. Its abiding truth is theological: God intends to dwell fully, gloriously, and in perfect order among a purified people.

debates.question: What kind of temple does this vision describe?

views.label: A literal future temple
views.case: Some read this as a literal architectural blueprint for a temple still to be built, whether in a future era of Israel's history or in a coming kingdom, understanding the measurements as intended for actual construction.

views.label: A symbol of God's ordered, holy presence
views.case: Others read the vision primarily as a symbolic assurance that God's presence will return in perfect order and holiness, using real-seeming architectural detail to convey a spiritual truth rather than a construction plan.

views.label: Typological, fulfilled in Christ and the church
views.case: Others see the temple as a type whose substance is Christ Himself, the true temple, John 2:19-21, extended to the church as God's dwelling, Ephesians 2:19-22, and consummated in Revelation 21:22, where the new Jerusalem has no temple because God and the Lamb are its temple.

debates.bearing: Faithful, orthodox readers hold each of these views, and this study does not adjudicate between them but commends interpretive humility. The New Testament clearly moves toward Christ and the church as fulfillment of the temple hope, without necessarily excluding a future dimension some readers still anticipate.

debates.question: Why does the vision dwell so long on precise measurements?

views.label: To assure the exiles that restoration is definite, not vague
views.case: Precise numbers, repeated again and again, function like a surveyor's promise: this is not wishful thinking but a plan already fixed in the mind of God.

views.label: To establish an ordered pattern of holiness for worship
views.case: The careful gradation from outer court to inner court to Most Holy Place teaches Israel, and every later reader, that approach to God is real, structured, and never casual.

debates.bearing: Both purposes work together: the specificity is pastoral, giving exiles confidence, and instructive, teaching reverence for God's holiness.

study.originalLanguages: The guide carries a qaneh, a measuring reed, the very instrument of a God who leaves nothing to chance in His plans to dwell with His people. Carved kerubim, cherubim, alternate with palm trees on wall after wall, echoing the guardians of Eden and of the ark. The vision builds steadily toward one word, qodesh, holy, set apart, naming the chambers, the offerings, and the mountain itself, and it closes by fixing its opposite, chol, the common, at a permanent distance from it.

typology.type: The measuring rod that fixes every dimension of the temple
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the measuring of the new Jerusalem with a golden reed in Revelation 21:15-17, where God's dwelling with His people is finally, fully realized.

typology.type: The carved cherubim guarding the sanctuary
typology.fulfillment: Echoes the cherubim who guarded Eden's entrance, Genesis 3:24, and anticipates a house where access to God's presence is no longer barred but opened in Christ.

typology.type: The whole measured, graded structure of courts, chambers, and the Most Holy Place
typology.fulfillment: Points to Christ, the true temple, John 2:19-21, to the church built together as God's dwelling, Ephesians 2:19-22, and to the new Jerusalem, which needs no temple at all, Revelation 21:22.

study.christ: John records Jesus pointing to His own body as the true temple, 'destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up,' John 2:19-21, so that Ezekiel's measured house finds its deepest fulfillment not in stone but in a person. Paul then extends the pattern to the church, 'built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit,' Ephesians 2:19-22, and John finally sees a city with no temple at all, 'because the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb are its temple,' Revelation 21:22. The measuring rod comes full circle: from a house built by hands to a house that is Christ Himself, and to a city where His presence fills everything.
study.god: God is a God of order, not chaos, taking such evident care over gates, chambers, tables, and thresholds that no detail is beneath His notice. He is also a God who guards holiness fiercely, since the entire vision moves toward a single line, 'to separate the holy from the common' (42:20), yet the vision's whole purpose is to prepare a way for Him to dwell among His people again.

crossReferences.note: Solomon's temple, the pattern this vision both echoes and surpasses in scale and detail.
Jesus identifies His own body as the true temple that this vision anticipates.
The church is built together as a dwelling place for God, extending the temple pattern to God's gathered people.
An angel measures the new Jerusalem with a rod, and there is no temple there, for God and the Lamb are its temple.

study.oneStory: From the tabernacle in the wilderness to Solomon's temple to this measured vision, Scripture keeps returning to one question: how can a holy God dwell among an unholy people? Ezekiel's temple answers with careful, hopeful detail, and the rest of the Bible narrows that answer to one person, Christ, in whom God's presence permanently dwells, John 1:14, before opening out again into the whole church and finally into a new creation where every corner is holy and no temple building is needed at all.

quiz.q: Who guides Ezekiel through the vision, and what does he carry?

options.text: The prophet Daniel, carrying a scroll
options.note: The guide is an unnamed, bronze-like figure, not Daniel.

options.text: An angel of fire, carrying a sword
options.note: The guide is described as bronze in appearance, and he carries a measuring rod, not a sword.

options.text: A man whose appearance was like bronze, carrying a linen cord and a measuring rod
options.note: Ezekiel 40:3 describes exactly this guide and his instruments.

options.text: No guide is mentioned; Ezekiel wanders alone
options.note: The guide is central to the entire vision from 40:3 onward.

quiz.q: What repeatedly happens as the guide moves through gate after gate and chamber after chamber?

options.text: He tells Ezekiel to measure it himself later
options.note: The guide himself does the measuring throughout.

options.text: He offers sacrifices at each location
options.note: Sacrifice regulations are described, but the guide's own repeated action throughout is measuring, not sacrificing.

options.text: He simply describes what he sees without measuring
options.note: He does describe the structures, but the vision's hallmark action is precise measurement, cubit by cubit.

options.text: He measures everything with painstaking precision, cubit by cubit
options.note: This precise, repeated measuring is the vision's defining feature from chapter 40 through 42.

quiz.q: What does the man call the innermost room, and what does he call the wooden altar?

options.text: He calls it 'the throne room' and the table 'the mercy seat'
options.note: Neither term appears; the correct names are 'the Most Holy Place' and 'the table that is before the LORD.'

options.text: No inner room or table is described in the vision
options.note: Both are explicitly described, in 41:4 and 41:22.

options.text: He calls it 'the ark room' and never mentions a table
options.note: The room is called the Most Holy Place, and a wooden altar is explicitly called 'the table that is before the LORD,' 41:22.

options.text: He calls the inner room 'the Most Holy Place' and the wooden altar 'the table that is before the LORD'
options.note: These exact titles appear in 41:4 and 41:22.

quiz.q: What decorates the temple walls throughout the inner and outer sanctuary?

options.text: Gold-plated images of the sun and stars
options.note: No such imagery is described; the carvings are cherubim and palm trees.

options.text: Alternating carved cherubim and palm trees
options.note: 41:18-20 and 41:25 describe this exact recurring pattern of carvings.

options.text: Bare, undecorated stone
options.note: The walls are extensively carved and paneled, not bare.

options.text: Painted scenes of Israel's history
options.note: The decoration described is carved cherubim and palm trees, not historical painted scenes.

quiz.q: What is the stated purpose of the great five-hundred-cubit measured square in 42:15-20?

options.text: To provide space for a marketplace
options.note: No marketplace purpose is given; the stated purpose is a holy boundary.

options.text: To mark the boundary of the city of Jerusalem itself
options.note: This square marks the temple area's boundary, not the wider city.

options.text: To measure land for future agricultural use
options.note: The text gives a different, explicitly stated purpose.

options.text: To separate the holy from the common
options.note: 42:20 states this purpose directly, in these exact terms.

questions: The vision spends chapter after chapter on measurements before a single sacrifice is offered. What might it mean for your own life that God cares this much about order and preparation before worship even begins?

The temple is graded from outer court to inner court to the Most Holy Place, each step more restricted and more sacred. How does this shape the way you think about approaching God today, now that Christ has opened that access, Hebrews 10:19-22?

Carved cherubim, once guardians barring the way back to Eden, now decorate the walls of God's house rather than blocking its entrance. Where do you need to be reminded that in Christ, the way to God is open rather than guarded against you?

The vision insists on separating 'the holy from the common' (42:20). Where in your own life have holy and common become blurred, and what would it look like to restore a clearer sense of what is set apart for God?

This study holds several faithful views on what this temple ultimately is. How does practicing humility over disputed details, while holding firmly to Christ as the true temple, shape the way you read difficult prophetic passages?

translationNotes.note: The 'long cubit' here is specified as a standard cubit plus a handbreadth, roughly twenty and a half inches rather than the more common eighteen-inch cubit; the text defines its own unit before using it throughout the vision.
'Most Holy Place' translates the Hebrew for 'holy of holies,' the same phrase used for the innermost room of the tabernacle and of Solomon's temple.

The description of the side rooms widening at each level upward is among the most difficult architectural Hebrew in the Bible, and translations differ on the exact structural detail; the main point, an ascending, expanding structure around the temple, is clear even where particulars remain debated.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 40:1-42:20. Em dashes in 40:1, 40:41, and 42:13 replaced with commas; stray spaces before closing parentheses and quotation marks in 40:30 and 41:4 tidied. No em or en dashes used. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was found addressing this passage directly this session, so sermon status is none.
